24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~REF below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (Definition~REF and Lemma~REF),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~REF),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~REF),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~REF), and
54	  \item the corollary regular \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~REF).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~REF) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~REF to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
60	
61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	
66	\leanok
67	  [Depth of a module]
68	  \label{def:depth}
69	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
70	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
71	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
72	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
73	  % "Let \(R\) be a ring, and \(I \subset R\) an ideal. Let \(M\) be a finite \(R\)-module.
74	  %  The {\it \(I\)-depth} of \(M\), denoted \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is defined as follows:
75	  %  \begin{enumerate}
76	  %  \item if \(IM \not = M\), then \(\text{depth}_I(M)\) is the supremum in
77	  %  \(\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences in \(I\),
78	  %  \item if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\).
79	  %  \end{enumerate}
80	  %  If \((R, \mathfrak m)\) is local we call \(\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)\) simply
81	  %  the {\it depth} of \(M\)."
82	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
83	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(I \subseteq R\) be an ideal, and let \(M\) be a finitely
84	  generated \(R\)-module. The \emph{\(I\)-depth} of \(M\), written \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is the
85	  supremum in \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences contained
86	  in \(I\), provided \(IM \neq M\); if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\). When
87	  \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
88	  \(\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)\) and call this simply the
89	  \emph{depth} of \(M\). The depth of the ring \(R\) itself is \(\text{depth}(R)\), the depth
90	  of \(R\) as a module over itself.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	Recall that a sequence \(f_1, \dots, f_r \in R\) is called \emph{\(M\)-regular} if
94	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0\) and for each \(i\), the element \(f_i\) is a non-zero-divisor on
95	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M\). The empty sequence is regular on \(M\) iff \(M \neq 0\) (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention \(\text{depth}(0) = \infty\)
97	above is the usual one).
98	
99	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the \(\text{Ext}\) characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
102	
103	\begin{lemma}
104	
105	\leanok
106	  [Depth via \(\text{Ext}\)]
107	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
108	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
109	  
110	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
111	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\).
114	  %  Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module. Then \(\text{depth}(M)\)
115	  %  is equal to the smallest integer \(i\) such that
116	  %  \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\) is nonzero."
117	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
118	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
119	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}\) and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely generated \(R\)-module. Then
120	  \[
121	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
122	  \]
123	\end{lemma}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
126	% "Let \(\delta(M)\) denote the depth of \(M\) and let \(i(M)\) denote
127	%  the smallest integer \(i\) such that \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\)
128	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that \(i(M) < \infty\).
129	%  By Lemma REF we have
130	%  \(\delta(M) = 0\) if and only if \(i(M) = 0\), because
131	%  \(\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)\) exactly means
132	%  that \(i(M) = 0\). Hence if \(\delta(M)\) or \(i(M)\) is \(> 0\), then we may
133	%  choose \(x \in \mathfrak m\) such that (a) \(x\) is a nonzerodivisor
134	%  on \(M\), and (b) \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1\).
135	%  Consider the long exact sequence
136	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
137	%  \(0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0\) by Lemma REF:
138	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
139	%  Since \(x \in \mathfrak m\) all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
140	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
141	%  Lemma REF.
142	%  Thus it is clear that \(i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1\). Induction on
143	%  \(\delta(M)\) finishes the proof."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	  Write \(\delta := \text{depth}(M)\) and \(i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}\).
148	  The base case \(\delta = 0\) is exactly \(\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0\) (some element of
149	  \(M\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\), i.e.\ \(\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)\), i.e.\
150	  \(\mathfrak{m}\) contains a zero-divisor on \(M\)), which is \(i = 0\). For the inductive step
151	  pick \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) a non-zero-divisor on \(M\) with
152	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1\). The short exact sequence
153	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0\) induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
154	  -)\(. Multiplication by \)x\( annihilates each \)\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
155	  \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) and \(\kappa\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\)), so the long exact
156	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
157	  \(\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)\) at the relevant
158	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by \(1\). By induction
159	  \(i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1\), so \(i = \delta\).
160	\end{proof}
161	
162	\section{Projective dimension}
163	\label{sec:ab_pd}
164	
165	\begin{definition}
166	
167	\leanok
168	  [Projective dimension]
169	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
170	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
171	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
172	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
173	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
174	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
175	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
176	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
177	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module. The \emph{projective
178	  dimension} of \(M\), written \(\text{pd}_R(M)\), is the infimum in
179	  \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths \(n\) of projective resolutions
180	  \[
181	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
182	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
183	  \]
184	  with each \(P_i\) a projective \(R\)-module. We say \(M\) has \emph{finite projective dimension}
185	  if \(\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty\), and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
186	  convention \(\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty\) (some references); we use \(\text{pd}_R(0) = 0\) here
187	  (the empty resolution \(0 \to 0\) of length \(0\)), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
188	  Theorem~REF concerns only nonzero \(M\).
189	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated \(R\)-module of finite projective
190	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each \(P_i\)
191	  free of finite rank and each transition map \(P_{i+1} \to P_i\) landing in \(\mathfrak{m} P_i\).
192	\end{definition}
193	
194	The two basic facts about \(\text{pd}_R(M)\) needed downstream are:
195	\begin{itemize}
196	  \item \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) iff \(M\) is projective (in the local Noetherian setting,
197	    iff \(M\) is free);
198	  \item if \(0 \to K \to P \to M \to 0\) is a short exact sequence with \(P\) projective, then
199	    \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_R(K) + 1\) provided \(K \neq 0\).
200	\end{itemize}
201	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under
202	\texttt{Module.projectiveDimension\_succ\_iff} or similar in Mathlib.
203	
204	\section{Depth on a short exact sequence}
205	\label{sec:ab_depth_ses}
206	
207	\begin{lemma}
208	
209	\leanok
210	  [Depth on a short exact sequence]
211	  \label{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
212	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_of_short_exact}
213	  
214	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses)
215	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17887-L17920).
216	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
217	  % "Let \(R\) be a local Noetherian ring. Let \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\)
218	  %  be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite \(R\)-modules.
219	  %  \begin{enumerate}
220	  %  \item
221	  %  \(\text{depth}(N) \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N'), \text{depth}(N'')\}\)
222	  %  \item
223	  %  \(\text{depth}(N'') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N') - 1\}\)
224	  %  \item
225	  %  \(\text{depth}(N') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N'') + 1\}\)
226	  %  \end{enumerate}"
227	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses).}
228	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let
229	  \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of nonzero finitely generated
230	  \(R\)-modules. Then
231	  \begin{enumerate}
232	    \item \(\text{depth}(N) \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N'),\; \text{depth}(N'')\bigr)\);
233	    \item \(\text{depth}(N'') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N') - 1\bigr)\);
234	    \item \(\text{depth}(N') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N'') + 1\bigr)\).
235	  \end{enumerate}
236	\end{lemma}
237	
238	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
239	% "Use the characterization of depth using the Ext groups
240	%  \(\Ext^i(\kappa, N)\), see Lemma REF,
241	%  and use the long exact cohomology sequence
242	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
243	%  from Lemma REF."
244	\begin{proof}
245	  
246	  
247	  By Lemma~REF, \(\text{depth}(N')\) (resp.\ \(\text{depth}(N)\),
248	  \(\text{depth}(N'')\)) is the smallest index at which \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) is
249	  nonzero on \(N'\) (resp.\ \(N\), \(N''\)). Apply the long exact \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\)
250	  sequence to \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\):
251	  \[
252	    \cdots \to \text{Ext}^{i-1}_R(\kappa, N'')
253	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N')
254	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N)
255	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N'')
256	    \to \text{Ext}^{i+1}_R(\kappa, N') \to \cdots
257	  \]
258	  Each of the three inequalities is then a direct read-off of the fact that if two
259	  of the three modules in a length-3 chunk vanish then the middle vanishes (for (1)) /
260	  the boundary terms vanish (for (2),(3)). Compare with the diagonal vanishing argument in
261	  the proof of Lemma~REF.
262	\end{proof}
263	
264	\section{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula}
265	\label{sec:ab_main}
266	
267	\begin{theorem}
268	
269	\leanok
270	  [Auslander--Buchsbaum formula]
271	  \label{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
272	  \lean{RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula}
273	  
274	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum)
275	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L27207-L27280).
276	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
277	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring. Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module
278	  %  which has finite projective dimension \(\text{pd}_R(M)\). Then we have
279	  %  \(\)
280	  %  \text{depth}(R) = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)
281	  %  \(\)"
282	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum); cf.\
283	  Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Theorem 19.1; Auslander--Buchsbaum,
284	  \emph{Homological dimension in local rings}, 1957.}
285	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely
286	  generated \(R\)-module of finite projective dimension. Then
287	  \[
288	    \text{pd}_R(M) \;+\; \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \text{depth}(R).
289	  \]
290	\end{theorem}
291	
292	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
293	% "We prove this by induction on \(\text{depth}(M)\). The most interesting
294	%  case is the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\). In this case, let
295	%  \(\)
296	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
297	%  \(\)
298	%  be a minimal finite free resolution, so \(e = \text{pd}_R(M)\).
299	%  By Lemma REF we may assume all matrix
300	%  coefficients of the maps in the complex are contained in the maximal
301	%  ideal of \(R\). Then on the one hand, by
302	%  Proposition REF we see that
303	%  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, breaking the long
304	%  exact sequence into short exact sequences
305	%  \begin{align*}
306	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e - 1}} \to K_{e - 2} \to 0,\\
307	%  0 \to K_{e - 2} \to R^{n_{e - 2}} \to K_{e - 3} \to 0,\\
308	%  \ldots,\\
309	%  0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
310	%  \end{align*}
311	%  we see, using Lemma REF, that
312	%  \begin{align*}
313	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1,\\
314	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2,\\
315	%  \ldots,\\
316	%  \text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e - 1),\\
317	%  \text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e
318	%  \end{align*}
319	%  and since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we conclude \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\).
320	%  This finishes the proof of the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).
321	%
322	%  Induction step. If \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), then we pick \(x \in \mathfrak m\)
323	%  which is a nonzerodivisor on both \(M\) and \(R\). This is possible, because
324	%  either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\) and \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the aforementioned
325	%  Proposition REF or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which
326	%  case \(M\) is finite free hence also \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\).
327	%  Thus \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by Lemma REF
328	%  (for example) and we can find an \(x \in \mathfrak m\) which is a nonzerodivisor
329	%  on both \(R\) and \(M\). Let
330	%  \(\)
331	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
332	%  \(\)
333	%  be a minimal resolution as above. An application of the snake lemma
334	%  shows that
335	%  \(\)
336	%  0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to
337	%  M/xM \to 0
338	%  \(\)
339	%  is a minimal resolution too. Thus \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\).
340	%  By Lemma REF we have
341	%  \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
342	%  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\).
343	%  Till now depths have all been depths as \(R\) modules, but we observe that
344	%  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) and similarly for \(R/xR\).
345	%  By induction hypothesis we see that the
346	%  Auslander-Buchsbaum formula holds for \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\). Since the
347	%  depths of both \(R/xR\) and \(M/xM\) have decreased by one and the projective
348	%  dimension has not changed we conclude."
349	\begin{proof}
350	  
351	  We induct on \(\text{depth}(M)\).
352	
353	  \emph{Base case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).} Let \(e := \text{pd}_R(M)\) and pick a minimal
354	  finite free resolution
355	  \[
356	    0 \longrightarrow R^{n_e} \longrightarrow R^{n_{e-1}} \longrightarrow
357	    \cdots \longrightarrow R^{n_0} \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
358	  \]
359	  with all transition maps having matrix coefficients in \(\mathfrak{m}\) (this is the
360	  ``minimal-resolution-after-trimming-trivial-summands'' normalisation, cf.\ Stacks
361	  \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}). On the one hand, the ``what is exact'' criterion
362	  (Stacks tag 00MF, \texttt{proposition-what-exact}, expressing exactness of a sequence of
363	  finite free modules in terms of depths of ideals of \(r\)-minors) gives
364	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, splitting the resolution into short exact
365	  sequences
366	  \[
367	    0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to K_{e-2} \to 0, \quad
368	    0 \to K_{e-2} \to R^{n_{e-2}} \to K_{e-3} \to 0, \quad \dots, \quad
369	    0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0,
370	  \]
371	  and applying Lemma~REF iteratively,
372	  yields the chain of inequalities
373	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1\),
374	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2\), \dots,
375	  \(\text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e-1)\), and finally
376	  \(\text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e\). Since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we deduce
377	  \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\). Combined with the previous inequality, $\text{depth}(R) = e =
378	  \text{pd}_R(M) + 0 = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)$.
379	
380	  \emph{Inductive step \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\).} We claim there exists \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\)
381	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both \(R\) and \(M\). Indeed: either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\), in
382	  which case \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
383	  of a minimal resolution, or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which case \(M\) is finite free and
384	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\). Either way both \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) and
385	  \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), hence \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by
386	  Lemma~REF, so a common non-zero-divisor $x \in
387	  \mathfrak{m}\( exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of \)M$ as above; the snake
388	  lemma applied to multiplication by \(x\) gives a minimal finite free resolution
389	  \[
390	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
391	  \]
392	  of \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\), of the same length \(e\). So
393	  \(\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\). By Stacks
394	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
395	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\), and one verifies
396	  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) (regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}\)
397	  correspond to regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}/(x)\)). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
398	  \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\):
399	  \[
400	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
401	  \]
402	  Substituting yields
403	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\), i.e.\
404	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)\), as required.
405	\end{proof}

407	\section{Corollary: regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay}
408	\label{sec:ab_regular_cm}
409	
410	\begin{definition}
411	
412	\leanok
413	  [Cohen--Macaulay local ring]
414	  \label{def:cohen_macaulay_local}
415	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay}
416	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM)
417	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25288-L25299).
418	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
419	  % "A Noetherian local ring \(R\) is called {\it Cohen-Macaulay}
420	  %  if it is Cohen-Macaulay as a module over itself."
421	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM).}
422	  A Noetherian local ring \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is called \emph{Cohen--Macaulay} if
423	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\) (equivalently, if \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay as a module
424	  over itself, where Cohen--Macaulay for a finite \(R\)-module \(M\) means
425	  \(\dim(\text{Supp}(M)) = \text{depth}(M)\), cf.\ Stacks tag 00N3).
426	\end{definition}
427	
428	% NOTE (iter-185 review, supersedes iter-184): the Lean assembly
429	% `CohenMacaulay.of_regular` calls a non-private helper
430	% `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` (AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean
431	% L1096 — line drifted from iter-184's L944 reference) that now carries a
432	% structural induction scaffold via `regularLocal_inductive_step`, with the
433	% load-bearing sorry pushed into two named helpers: (i)
434	% `exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ` consolidating the
435	% Stacks 00NQ + 00NU substrate gaps in one signature, and (ii) an inline
436	% technical-bridge sorry inside `regularLocal_inductive_step` for the
437	% `R⧸(x)`-vs-`QuotSMulTop x R` linear-equiv upgrade (~10-20 LOC iter-186 close
438	% via `Submodule.ideal_span_singleton_smul` + `QuotSMulTop.mem_annihilator`).
439	% Neither has a `\lean{...}` pin in this chapter (the lean-vs-blueprint-checker
440	% iter185-auslander minor flagged adding a pin for the public-facing
441	% `exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` itself; iter-186+ chapter touch can add
442	% that hint). The Stacks 00NQ Mathlib gap remains the load-bearing substrate;
443	% iter-186+ work paths unchanged: (a) formalise Stacks 00NQ in project
444	% (~300 LOC); (b) Koszul-homology bypass via `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index`;
445	% (c) Mathlib upstream.
446	\begin{corollary}
447	
448	\leanok
449	  [Regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay]
450	  \label{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
451	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular}
452	  
453	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM)
454	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25714-L25730).
455	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
456	  % "Let \(R\) be a regular local ring and let
457	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) be a minimal set of generators
458	  %  for the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\). Then
459	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) is a regular sequence, and
460	  %  each \(R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c)\) is a regular local ring
461	  %  of dimension \(d - c\). In particular \(R\) is Cohen-Macaulay."
462	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM).}
463	  Every regular Noetherian local ring is Cohen--Macaulay: if \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is
464	  regular local of Krull dimension \(d\), then \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\).
465	\end{corollary}
466	
467	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
468	% "Note that \(R/x_1R\) is a Noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\)
469	%  by Lemma REF with \(x_2, \ldots, x_d\)
470	%  generating the maximal ideal. Hence it is a regular local ring by definition.
471	%  Since \(R\) is a domain by Lemma REF
472	%  \(x_1\) is a nonzerodivisor."
473	\begin{proof}
474	  
475	  
476	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be regular of dimension \(d\) and pick a minimal generating set
477	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) of \(\mathfrak{m}\). By Stacks tag 00NQ (lemma-regular-domain), \(R\) is
478	  an integral domain, so \(x_1\) is a non-zero-divisor. The quotient \(R/x_1 R\) is a Noetherian
479	  local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\) (by Krull's principal ideal theorem,
480	  \texttt{lemma-one-equation}) with maximal ideal generated by \(x_2, \dots, x_d\), hence
481	  has dimension exactly \(d - 1\) and is again regular of dimension \(d - 1\). Inducting on \(d\),
482	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) is an \(R\)-regular sequence, so
483	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq d\). The reverse inequality \(\text{depth}(R) \leq \dim(R) = d\)
484	  always holds for any nonzero finite module over a Noetherian local ring
485	  (Stacks tag 00LK, \texttt{lemma-bound-depth}). Thus \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\), i.e.\
486	  \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay.
487	
488	  \emph{Remark on the Auslander--Buchsbaum route.} The same conclusion can be obtained from
489	  Theorem~REF as follows: take \(M := R\) itself. Then \(M\) is
490	  finitely generated and projective (free of rank \(1\)), so \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\). The
491	  formula gives \(\text{depth}(R) = 0 + \text{depth}(R)\), a tautology. The non-tautological
492	  use of Auslander--Buchsbaum is rather: for a regular local ring of dimension \(d\), every
493	  finitely generated module \(M\) has \(\text{pd}_R(M) \leq d\) (Stacks tag 00O2,
494	  \texttt{proposition-finite-gl-dim-regular}); applying the formula to such an \(M\) then
495	  relates \(\text{depth}(M)\) to \(d - \text{pd}_R(M)\), which is the form most often used in
496	  practice. The direct regular-sequence argument above is the cleanest proof of
497	  \(R\) Cohen--Macaulay itself.
498	\end{proof}

500	\section{Application: codim-1 extension on a regular surface (gating A.4.a)}
501	\label{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}
502	
503	The downstream consumer is A.4.a: extending a rational map
504	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from a regular projective surface \(S\) to an abelian variety \(A\)
505	across a closed point \(x \in S\) that is missing from the domain of definition \(U \subseteq S\).
506	The classical mechanism (cf.\ Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.7) is:
507	
508	\begin{enumerate}
509	  \item The local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) is a regular Noetherian local ring of Krull
510	    dimension \(2\) (because \(S\) is regular and \(\dim S = 2\)).
511	  \item By Corollary~REF it is Cohen--Macaulay, so
512	    \(\text{depth}(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}) = 2\). Equivalently, the maximal ideal contains an
513	    \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\)-regular sequence of length \(2\) (any two transverse generators).
514	  \item Sections of the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S\) on \(U = S \setminus \{x\}\) extend
515	    uniquely across \(x\) because \(H^0_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) and
516	    \(H^1_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) (these local-cohomology vanishings are equivalent to depth
517	    \(\geq 2\) at \(x\) by Stacks tag 0AVF; see Hartshorne, III.3.5, for the surface case).
518	  \item For a rational map \(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) with codimension-\(\geq 2\) indeterminacy
519	    locus on a regular surface, the same depth-\(\geq 2\) input produces a sheaf-theoretic
520	    extension of \(f\) to all of \(S\): pull back any sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_A\)-affine-locally,
521	    obtain sections over \(U\), and extend across \(x\) by the above.
522	\end{enumerate}
523	
524	So Corollary~REF is the gating input: the geometric A.4.a
525	proof can be written conditional on the algebraic Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary, and the
526	two halves develop independently --- as long as Theorem~REF and
527	Corollary~REF are formalized in
528	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} with the signatures pinned
529	in this chapter, A.4.a can consume them as Mathlib-style imports.

799	/-! ## §5. The Auslander–Buchsbaum formula
800	
801	For a nonzero finite module `M` of finite projective dimension over a
802	Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)`, the **Auslander–Buchsbaum formula** reads
803	```
804	  pd_R(M) + depth(M) = depth(R)
805	```
806	(Stacks tag 090V). The proof inducts on `depth(M)`: the base case
807	`depth(M) = 0` uses a minimal finite free resolution of `M` and the
808	"what is exact" criterion (Stacks 00MF) plus iterated application of the
809	depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma (§4) to bound `depth(R)` against the
810	resolution length; the inductive step picks a common non-zero-divisor
811	`x ∈ 𝔪` on both `R` and `M`, applies the snake lemma to obtain a minimal
812	finite free resolution of `M/xM` over `R/xR` of the same length, and uses
813	the inductive hypothesis on `M/xM` over `R/xR`.
814	
815	Blueprint reference: `thm:auslander_buchsbaum` (Stacks tag 090V). -/
816	
817	/-- **The Auslander–Buchsbaum formula.** Let `(R, 𝔪)` be a Noetherian local
818	ring and let `M` be a nonzero finite `R`-module of finite projective
819	dimension. Then
820	```
821	  pd_R(M) + depth_R(M) = depth(R).
822	```
823	
824	The hypothesis "finite projective dimension" is encoded by an explicit
825	upper bound `n : ℕ` on the projective dimension (so the formula compares
826	finite numeric quantities cleanly without `WithBot ℕ∞`-arithmetic
827	subtleties).
828	
829	iter-176+: the body is the induction on `depth(M)` outlined in the
830	chapter — base case via the minimal-finite-free-resolution +
831	"what is exact" + iterated `Module.depth_of_short_exact`; inductive step
832	via the snake lemma on multiplication by a common non-zero-divisor and
833	the inductive hypothesis on `M/xM` over `R/xR`. For the iter-175
834	file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
835	theorem auslander_buchsbaum_formula
836	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
837	    {M : Type u} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [_root_.Module.Finite R M]
838	    [Nontrivial M]
839	    (n : ℕ)
840	    (_hpd : _root_.Module.projectiveDimension R M = (n : WithBot ℕ∞)) :
841	    (n : ℕ∞) + Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M
842	      = Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R := by
843	  sorry
844	
845	/-! ## §6. Cohen–Macaulay local rings
846	
847	A Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` is **Cohen–Macaulay** if its depth equals
848	its Krull dimension (Stacks tag 00N4). Mathlib `b80f227` has neither the
849	predicate nor the class — this file is the upstream gap-fill.
850	
851	Blueprint reference: `def:cohen_macaulay_local` (Stacks tag 00N4). -/
852	
853	/-- A Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` is **Cohen–Macaulay** if its depth
854	equals its Krull dimension: `depth(R) = dim R`.
855	
856	Encoded as a `Prop`-valued type class so downstream consumers can write
857	`[CohenMacaulay R]` and use Cohen–Macaulay as a hypothesis. Mathlib at the
858	pinned commit (`b80f227`) does not expose any Cohen–Macaulay predicate;
859	this is the upstream gap-fill.
860	
861	iter-176+: the predicate is `Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R =
862	ringKrullDim R`. For the iter-175 file-skeleton the carrier definition is a
863	typed `sorry` at the `Prop` level — substantively, the predicate is the
864	named equality, but we package it as a `class` so use sites are uniform. -/
865	class CohenMacaulay (R : Type u) [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R]
866	    [IsNoetherianRing R] : Prop where
867	  /-- The Cohen–Macaulay equation: `depth(R) = ringKrullDim R`. The numeric
868	  comparison is in `WithBot ℕ∞` after coercion of the `ℕ∞`-valued depth. -/
869	  depth_eq_krullDim :
870	    (Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R : WithBot ℕ∞) = ringKrullDim R
871	
872	/-! ## §7. Regular local rings are Cohen–Macaulay
873	
874	The consumer-facing input for A.4.a: every regular Noetherian local ring is
875	Cohen–Macaulay (Stacks tag 00OD). The direct proof: pick a minimal
876	generating set `x_1, …, x_d` of `𝔪` (where `d = dim R`), use that `R` is a
877	domain (Stacks 00NQ) to start an `R`-regular sequence, and induct on
878	dimension — each `R/(x_1, …, x_c)` is again regular of dimension `d - c`,
879	so `x_1, …, x_d` is an `R`-regular sequence and `depth(R) ≥ d`. The reverse
880	inequality `depth(R) ≤ dim R` is the standard depth bound (Stacks 00LK).
881	
882	Blueprint reference: `cor:regular_cohen_macaulay` (Stacks tag 00OD). -/
883	
884	namespace CohenMacaulay
885	
886	/-! ### Helper 1 (axiom-clean): length-bound on regular sequences
887	
888	For a Noetherian local ring `R`, every `R`-regular sequence has length at most
889	`ringKrullDim R`. This is the **upper bound** half of Stacks 00OD: it is the
890	specialisation of the equality
891	`ringKrullDim (R / ofList rs) + rs.length = ringKrullDim R`
892	(`ringKrullDim_add_length_eq_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular`) to the observation that
893	`ringKrullDim (R / ofList rs) ≥ 0` whenever the quotient is nontrivial, which it
894	is precisely because `IsRegular` rules out `rs • ⊤ = ⊤`.
895	
896	iter-181 Lane G: closed kernel-clean. -/
897	private lemma length_le_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular
898	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
899	    {rs : List R} (h : RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular R rs) :
900	    (rs.length : WithBot ℕ∞) ≤ ringKrullDim R := by
901	  have heq := ringKrullDim_add_length_eq_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular rs h
902	  have hntq : Nontrivial (R ⧸ Ideal.ofList rs) := by
903	    rw [Ideal.Quotient.nontrivial_iff]
904	    intro habs
905	    apply h.top_ne_smul
906	    change (⊤ : Submodule R R) = (Ideal.ofList rs) • ⊤

932	/-! ### Helper 2.0 (iter-187 Lane G sub-lane G1): cotangent dim-drop on `R ⧸ (x)`
933	
934	**Statement.** For a Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` and `x ∈ 𝔪 \ 𝔪²`, the
935	cotangent space of `R / (x)` has dimension one less than that of `R`:
936	```
937	finrank κ' (CotangentSpace (R/(x))) + 1 = finrank κ (CotangentSpace R)
938	```
939	where `κ = R / 𝔪` and `κ' = (R/(x)) / 𝔪'` are the two residue fields
940	(canonically isomorphic via the natural quotient `R/𝔪 ≃ (R/(x))/𝔪'`).
941	
942	**Role.** This is the cotangent-space dim-drop building block for
943	`exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ` below: once Stacks 00NQ
944	(regular local ⟹ domain) is filled in, this dim-drop is what upgrades
945	`R/(x)` of dimension `k` back to `IsRegularLocalRing` via
946	`IsRegularLocalRing.iff_finrank_cotangentSpace`.
947	
948	**Proof structure** (Bourbaki-style via `Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank`):
949	1. The R-algebra map `π : R → R/(x)` induces a κ-linear surjection of
950	   cotangent spaces `f : 𝔪.Cotangent → 𝔪'.Cotangent` via
951	   `Ideal.mapCotangent_surjective_of_comap_eq` (which applies because
952	   `(maximalIdeal R).comap π = ker π ⊔ maximalIdeal R = (x) ⊔ 𝔪 = 𝔪`).
953	2. The kernel of `f` is the R-submodule generated by the image `x̄` of `x`
954	   in `𝔪.Cotangent` (via `Ideal.mapCotangent_ker_of_surjective` — the
955	   kernel is `(Submodule.comap 𝔪.subtype ((x) ⊓ 𝔪)).map 𝔪.toCotangent`
956	   which collapses to `Submodule.span R {x̄}` because `(x) ⊆ 𝔪`).
957	3. The R-action on 𝔪.Cotangent factors through κ (it's a κ-vector space),
958	   so this is also `Submodule.span κ {x̄}`.
959	4. `x ∉ 𝔪²` ⟺ `x̄ ≠ 0` (Helper 1.5), giving
960	   `finrank κ (Submodule.span κ {x̄}) = 1` via `finrank_span_singleton`.
961	5. Combining via `Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank` and the
962	   `LinearMap.quotKerEquivOfSurjective` isomorphism, we get the formula.
963	   The residue-field switch `κ → κ'` is handled by
964	   `IsLocalRing.spanFinrank_maximalIdeal_eq_finrank_cotangentSpace`, which
965	   reduces the κ-finrank to spanFinrank (a ring-theoretic invariant) and
966	   the spanFinrank-equality is purely numeric.
967	
968	**iter-187 deliverable**: structural scaffold with the assembled body left
969	as a single named typed sorry (the κ-subspace identification step). The
970	preparatory lemma `toCotangent_ne_zero_of_not_mem_sq` lands axiom-clean.
971	
972	**iter-188+** body fill-in: instantiate steps (1)-(5) above. Key Mathlib API:
973	* `Ideal.mapCotangent`, `Ideal.mapCotangent_surjective_of_comap_eq`,
974	  `Ideal.mapCotangent_ker_of_surjective` (Mathlib b80f227 ✓)
975	* `Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank` (Mathlib b80f227 ✓)
976	* `finrank_span_singleton` (Mathlib b80f227 ✓)
977	* `IsLocalRing.spanFinrank_maximalIdeal_eq_finrank_cotangentSpace`
978	  (Mathlib b80f227 ✓) — bridges κ-finrank and κ'-finrank via spanFinrank.
979	
980	The substantive ring-theoretic content is concentrated in the kernel
981	description (step 2) and the κ vs κ' identification (step 5); both
982	have axiom-clean Mathlib infrastructure but require a careful
983	multi-step instantiation. -/

1261	/-! ### Narrow substrate gap (Stacks 00NQ): regular local ring is a domain
1262	
1263	The Auslander–Buchsbaum-route closure of "regular local ring is Cohen–Macaulay"
1264	ultimately reduces to **Stacks 00NQ**: every regular local Noetherian ring
1265	is an integral domain. Mathlib at the pinned commit `b80f227` does not expose
1266	this implication (`lean_leansearch` for "regular local ring is a domain"

1270	Stacks 00NQ proof sketch (induction on `dim R = spanFinrank 𝔪`):
1271	* Base `dim R = 0`: then `𝔪 = ⊥`, hence `R` is a field, hence a domain.
1272	* Step `dim R = d + 1 ≥ 1`:
1273	  - Pick `x ∈ 𝔪 \ 𝔪²` (axiom-clean via Nakayama; see
1274	    `exists_notMemSq_of_spanFinrank_pos` below).
1275	  - By the cotangent dim-drop (`finrank_cotangentSpace_quot_span_singleton_succ`,
1276	    iter-188 Lane G1 closure) **plus** Krull's principal ideal theorem
1277	    (`ringKrullDim_quotient_span_singleton_succ_eq_ringKrullDim_of_mem_jacobson`
1278	    gives the `≥` half), `R / (x)` is regular local of dim `d`.
1279	  - By induction, `R / (x)` is a domain, hence `(x)` is a prime ideal of `R`.
1280	  - Let `𝔭` be a minimal prime of `R` with `𝔭 ⊆ (x)`. For `y ∈ 𝔭`, write
1281	    `y = xz`; since `𝔭` is prime and `x ∉ 𝔭` (else `x ∈ 𝔭 ⊆ (x)` doesn't
1282	    immediately give a contradiction but combined with `dim R/(x) = d` and
1283	    `x ∈ 𝔭` height-zero we get `𝔭 = 0` which is the conclusion), we get
1284	    `z ∈ 𝔭`. Hence `𝔭 ⊆ x 𝔭`, so iterating, `𝔭 ⊆ ∩ x^n 𝔭 ⊆ ∩ 𝔪^n = 0`
1285	    (Krull intersection). So `𝔭 = 0`, and `R` is a domain.

1296	private lemma exists_notMemSq_of_spanFinrank_pos
1297	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
1298	    (h : 0 < (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank) :
1299	    ∃ x ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R, x ∉ (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) ^ 2 := by

1301	  by_contra h_neg
1302	  push Not at h_neg
1303	  have h𝔪_le_sq : IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R ≤ (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) ^ 2 := h_neg
1304	  -- 𝔪² = 𝔪 • 𝔪.
1305	  have hsq : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R : Submodule R R) ^ 2
1306	      = (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) • (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R : Submodule R R) := by
1307	    rw [sq, ← Ideal.smul_eq_mul]
1308	  have h𝔪_fg : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R : Submodule R R).FG :=
1309	    Ideal.fg_of_isNoetherianRing _
1310	  -- Use Submodule.le_of_le_smul_of_le_jacobson_bot with N = ⊥.
1311	  have hjac : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) ≤ (⊥ : Ideal R).jacobson :=
1312	    IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal_le_jacobson _
1313	  have h_le_smul : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R : Submodule R R)
1314	      ≤ ⊥ ⊔ (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) •
1315	        (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R : Submodule R R) := by
1316	    rw [bot_sup_eq, ← hsq]; exact h𝔪_le_sq
1317	  have h𝔪_bot : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R : Submodule R R) ≤ ⊥ :=
1318	    Submodule.le_of_le_smul_of_le_jacobson_bot h𝔪_fg hjac h_le_smul
1319	  have h𝔪_eq_bot : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) = ⊥ := le_bot_iff.mp h𝔪_bot
1320	  have h_span : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank = 0 := by
1321	    rw [h𝔪_eq_bot]; exact Submodule.spanFinrank_bot

1705	/-- **Lane G inductive step (iter-185 structural scaffold).** Packages the
1706	inductive step of Stacks 00OD: given a regular local ring `R` of dimension
1707	`k + 1`, plus the inductive hypothesis at dimension `k` (universally
1708	quantified in the ring), produce a regular sequence of length `k + 1` in
1709	the maximal ideal of `R`.
1710	
1711	The body is **axiom-clean** modulo the typed-`sorry` substrate helper
1712	`exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ`. Assembly path:
1713	
1714	1. Helper extracts `x ∈ 𝔪` with `IsSMulRegular R x` AND `IsRegularLocalRing
1715	   (R⧸(x))` of `spanFinrank = k`.
1716	2. IH applied on `R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}` produces a regular sequence
1717	   `rs'_q : List (R ⧸ (x))` of length `k` in the maximal ideal there.
1718	3. Lift `rs'_q` to `rs : List R` via `Function.surjInv` of
1719	   `Ideal.Quotient.mk_surjective`; the section property gives
1720	   `rs.map (Ideal.Quotient.mk _) = rs'_q`.
1721	4. Members of `rs` lie in `𝔪` because the maximal ideal of `R⧸(x)` is the
1722	   image of `𝔪` (`IsLocalRing.le_maximalIdeal` applied to the comap chain).
1723	5. Cons via `RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular.cons'` to form the length-`(k+1)`
1724	   sequence `x :: rs`.
1725	
1726	This factoring isolates the Mathlib gap to one narrow typed `sorry` instead of
1727	spreading it across the lemma body; future iterations can attack Stacks 00NQ
1728	+ 00NU directly. -/
1729	private lemma regularLocal_inductive_step {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R]
1730	    [IsNoetherianRing R] [IsRegularLocalRing R] {k : ℕ}
1731	    (hdim : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank = k + 1)
1732	    (IH : ∀ (R' : Type u) [CommRing R'] [IsLocalRing R'] [IsNoetherianRing R']
1733	            [IsRegularLocalRing R'],
1734	            (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R').spanFinrank = k →
1735	            ∃ rs : List R', rs.length = k ∧
1736	              (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R') ∧
1737	              RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular R' rs) :
1738	    ∃ rs : List R, rs.length = k + 1 ∧
1739	      (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) ∧
1740	      RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular R rs := by
1741	  -- Step 1: extract `x ∈ 𝔪` regular on `R` with `R ⧸ (x)` regular local of
1742	  -- `spanFinrank = k`.  This is the consolidated Stacks 00NQ + 00NU substrate.
1743	  obtain ⟨x, hxMem, hxReg, hRLR, hdim_quot⟩ :=
1744	    exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ hdim
1745	  -- Step 2: apply IH on `R ⧸ (x)` — this gives a regular sequence of length
1746	  -- `k` on the quotient, valued in its maximal ideal.
1747	  obtain ⟨rs'_q, hlen_q, hmem_q, hreg_q⟩ := IH (R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}) hdim_quot
1748	  -- Step 3: lift `rs'_q : List (R ⧸ (x))` to `rs' : List R` via the right
1749	  -- inverse of the (surjective) quotient ring hom.
1750	  let mkq : R →+* R ⧸ Ideal.span {x} := Ideal.Quotient.mk _
1751	  let g : R ⧸ Ideal.span {x} → R := Function.surjInv Ideal.Quotient.mk_surjective
1752	  have hg : ∀ y, mkq (g y) = y := Function.surjInv_eq _
1753	  let rs' : List R := rs'_q.map g
1754	  have hlen_rs' : rs'.length = k := by simp [rs', hlen_q]
1755	  have hmkmap : rs'.map mkq = rs'_q := by
1756	    change (rs'_q.map g).map mkq = rs'_q
1757	    rw [List.map_map]
1758	    conv_rhs => rw [← List.map_id rs'_q]
1759	    exact List.map_congr_left fun y _ => hg y
1760	  -- Step 4: each element of `rs'` lies in `𝔪 R` via the comap of `𝔪 (R⧸(x))`.
1761	  -- The maximal ideal of `R ⧸ (x)` comaps back to `𝔪 R` (it's the *unique*
1762	  -- maximal ideal of `R` containing `Ideal.span {x} ⊆ 𝔪`).
1763	  have hmem_rs' : ∀ r ∈ rs', r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R := by
1764	    intro r hr
1765	    simp only [rs', List.mem_map] at hr
1766	    obtain ⟨y, hy_mem, rfl⟩ := hr
1767	    -- The comap of `𝔪 (R⧸(x))` under the surjective `mkq` is a maximal ideal
1768	    -- of `R`; since `R` is local, it equals `𝔪 R`.  Hence `g y ∈ comap mkq 𝔪'`
1769	    -- iff `g y ∈ 𝔪 R`.
1770	    have hmax_comap : (Ideal.comap mkq
1771	        (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal (R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}))).IsMaximal :=
1772	      Ideal.comap_isMaximal_of_surjective _ Ideal.Quotient.mk_surjective
1773	    have heq : Ideal.comap mkq
1774	        (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal (R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}))
1775	        = IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R :=
1776	      (IsLocalRing.isMaximal_iff R).mp hmax_comap
1777	    rw [← heq, Ideal.mem_comap]
1778	    exact (hg y).symm ▸ hmem_q y hy_mem
1779	  -- Step 5: cons `x` onto `rs'` to form the length-`(k+1)` regular sequence.
1780	  refine ⟨x :: rs', ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
1781	  · simp [hlen_rs']
1782	  · intro r hr
1783	    rcases List.mem_cons.mp hr with rfl | hr_in
1784	    · exact hxMem
1785	    · exact hmem_rs' r hr_in
1786	  · -- `IsRegular R (x :: rs')` via `IsRegular.cons'`.
1787	    -- Need `IsSMulRegular R x` (have `hxReg`) AND
1788	    -- `IsRegular (QuotSMulTop x R) (rs'.map (Ideal.Quotient.mk (Ideal.span {x})))`.
1789	    refine RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular.cons' hxReg ?_
1790	    -- After `cons'`: goal is `IsRegular (QuotSMulTop x R) (rs'.map mkq)`
1791	    -- = `IsRegular (QuotSMulTop x R) rs'_q` (by `hmkmap`), implicit ring
1792	    -- `R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}` (inferred from list type).
1793	    rw [hmkmap]
1794	    -- Goal: `IsRegular (QuotSMulTop x R) rs'_q` (implicit ring `R ⧸ (x)`).
1795	    -- IH provides: `IsRegular (R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}) rs'_q` (same implicit
1796	    -- ring, but M differs: `R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}` vs `QuotSMulTop x R = R ⧸ (x • ⊤)`).
1797	    --
1798	    -- **iter-185 typed `sorry` — TECHNICAL BRIDGE (NOT a Mathlib substrate gap).**
1799	    -- The two M's are *equal as sets* — both are the quotient of `R` by the
1800	    -- principal ideal `(x)`, written two different ways:
1801	    --   * `R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}` — quotient by `Ideal.span {x}` as a Submodule R R;
1802	    --   * `QuotSMulTop x R = R ⧸ (x • ⊤)` — quotient by `x • ⊤` as a Submodule R R.
1803	    -- Mathlib's `Submodule.ideal_span_singleton_smul` gives
1804	    -- `(x • ⊤ : Submodule R R) = Ideal.span {x} • ⊤ = Ideal.span {x}` (the
1805	    -- second equality because `I • ⊤_R = I` for an ideal `I` of `R`).  The
1806	    -- bridge between the two M's is an `R⧸(x)`-linear equivalence; future iter
1807	    -- can construct this in ~10-20 LOC via:
1808	    --   (a) `Submodule.quotEquivOfEq` from the submodule equality (gives R-linear);
1809	    --   (b) upgrade to `R⧸(x)`-linear via `QuotSMulTop.mem_annihilator`
1810	    --       (since `x ∈ Module.annihilator R (QuotSMulTop x R)`, the R-action
1811	    --       factors through `R⧸(x)`, and any R-linear equiv between two such
1812	    --       modules is automatically `R⧸(x)`-linear);
1813	    --   (c) close via `LinearEquiv.isRegular_congr` on this `R⧸(x)`-linear equiv.
1814	    -- This bridge has no substrate dependencies — it's pure bookkeeping.
1815	    --
1816	    -- iter-186 Lane G: closed kernel-clean via the explicit `R⧸(x)`-linear
1817	    -- equiv between the two quotients, then `LinearEquiv.isRegular_congr`
1818	    -- transports `hreg_q` across.  The two `mapQ` halves use `LinearMap.id`
1819	    -- with `heq.le` / `heq.ge`, and `map_smul'` reduces to `rfl` after
1820	    -- `Quotient.inductionOn` on the scalar (the `R⧸(x)`-action on both sides
1821	    -- is `[s] • [r] = [s * r]` definitionally).
1822	    open scoped Pointwise in
1823	    have heq : (x • (⊤ : Submodule R R)) = (Ideal.span {x} : Submodule R R) := by
1824	      ext y
1825	      simp [Submodule.mem_smul_pointwise_iff_exists, Ideal.mem_span_singleton,
1826	        eq_comm, Dvd.dvd]
1827	    let e : (R ⧸ (x • (⊤ : Submodule R R))) ≃ₗ[R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}]
1828	        (R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}) := {
1829	      toFun := Submodule.mapQ _ _ LinearMap.id heq.le
1830	      invFun := Submodule.mapQ _ _ LinearMap.id heq.ge
1831	      left_inv := by rintro ⟨r⟩; rfl
1832	      right_inv := by rintro ⟨r⟩; rfl
1833	      map_add' := map_add _
1834	      map_smul' := by
1835	        rintro q ⟨r⟩
1836	        induction q using Quotient.inductionOn with
1837	        | _ s => rfl
1838	    }
1839	    exact (LinearEquiv.isRegular_congr e.symm rs'_q).mp hreg_q

1841	lemma exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal
1842	    (R : Type u) [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
1843	    [IsRegularLocalRing R] :
1844	    ∃ rs : List R, rs.length = (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank
1845	        ∧ (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R)
1846	        ∧ RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular R rs := by
1847	  -- Strong induction on `n = spanFinrank R`, generalising `R` so the inductive
1848	  -- hypothesis can be applied to the quotient `R/(x)` at smaller dimension.
1849	  suffices haux : ∀ (n : ℕ) (R : Type u) [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R]
1850	      [IsNoetherianRing R] [IsRegularLocalRing R],
1851	      (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank = n →
1852	      ∃ rs : List R, rs.length = n ∧
1853	        (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) ∧
1854	        RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular R rs by
1855	    exact haux _ R rfl
1856	  intro n
1857	  induction n with
1858	  | zero =>
1859	    -- Base case `dim 0`: spanFinrank = 0, hence `maximalIdeal R = ⊥` (so `R` is
1860	    -- a field). The empty list is trivially `R`-regular on the nonzero ring `R`.
1861	    intros R _ _ _ _ _hdim
1862	    refine ⟨[], rfl, by simp, ?_⟩
1863	    exact RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular.nil R R
1864	  | succ k ih =>
1865	    -- Inductive case `dim (k + 1)`: delegate to `regularLocal_inductive_step`,
1866	    -- supplying the inductive hypothesis at dimension `k`. The helper handles
1867	    -- the substantive NZD-extraction + quotient-regularity + cons assembly.
1868	    intros R _ _ _ _ hdim
1869	    exact regularLocal_inductive_step (k := k) hdim (fun R' _ _ _ _ h => ih R' h)
1870	
1871	/-- **Regular local rings are Cohen–Macaulay.** Every regular Noetherian
1872	local ring is Cohen–Macaulay: a minimal generating set of `𝔪` is an
1873	`R`-regular sequence of length `dim R`, so `depth(R) ≥ dim R`; combined
1874	with the standard upper bound `depth(R) ≤ dim R` (Stacks 00LK) this gives
1875	`depth(R) = dim R`.
1876	
1877	This is the consumer-facing input for **A.4.a** (codim-1 extension of a
1878	rational map across a codim-2 closed point on a regular projective
1879	surface): the local ring `O_{S,x}` of a regular projective surface at a
1880	closed point is regular of Krull dimension `2`, hence Cohen–Macaulay,
1881	hence has depth `2`, which is exactly the input the local-cohomology
1882	vanishing `H^i_x(O_S) = 0` for `i < 2` needs (Stacks 0AVF; see Hartshorne
1883	III.7).
1884	
1885	iter-181 Lane G **STRUCTURAL**: body is now decomposed into two
1886	typed helper lemmas — `length_le_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular` (the upper
1887	bound, closed kernel-clean from
1888	`ringKrullDim_add_length_eq_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular`) and
1889	`exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` (the lower bound, typed `sorry` on the
1890	Mathlib gap `IsRegularLocalRing ⟹ IsDomain` + regular-quotient induction).
1891	The combined assembly into `depth = ringKrullDim` is closed inline below,
1892	so the only residual `sorry` in this `instance` body is the named helper. -/
1893	instance of_regular (R : Type u) [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R]
1894	    [IsNoetherianRing R] [IsRegularLocalRing R] : CohenMacaulay R where
1895	  depth_eq_krullDim := by
1896	    -- Step 1: simplify `Module.depth` via the `else` branch
1897	    --   (since `𝔪 • ⊤ = 𝔪 ≠ ⊤` for a local ring's maximal ideal).
1898	    have h𝔪 : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) • (⊤ : Submodule R R)
1899	        ≠ (⊤ : Submodule R R) := by
1900	      have heq : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) • (⊤ : Submodule R R)
1901	          = IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R := by simp
1902	      rw [heq]
1903	      exact (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal.isMaximal R).ne_top
1904	    rw [Module.depth, if_neg h𝔪]
1905	    -- Step 2: convert RHS to the spanFinrank using
1906	    -- `IsRegularLocalRing.spanFinrank_maximalIdeal`.
1907	    rw [← IsRegularLocalRing.spanFinrank_maximalIdeal]
1908	    -- Goal: ((sSup {n | …} : ℕ∞) : WithBot ℕ∞)
1909	    --         = ((spanFinrank 𝔪 : ℕ) : WithBot ℕ∞)
1910	    -- Step 3: it suffices to show the sSup equals spanFinrank as ℕ∞,
1911	    -- via antisymmetry: upper bound from Helper 1, lower bound from Helper 2.
1912	    have h1 : (sSup { n : ℕ∞ | ∃ rs : List R, (rs.length : ℕ∞) = n ∧
1913	        (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R)
1914	          ∧ RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular R rs }
1915	        : ℕ∞) = ((IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank : ℕ∞) := by
1916	      apply le_antisymm
1917	      · -- Upper bound: every element of the sSup-set is at most spanFinrank.
1918	        apply sSup_le
1919	        rintro n ⟨rs, rfl, _, hreg⟩
1920	        have hub := length_le_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular hreg
1921	        rw [← IsRegularLocalRing.spanFinrank_maximalIdeal] at hub
1922	        exact_mod_cast hub
1923	      · -- Lower bound: spanFinrank is achieved by Helper 2's regular sequence.
1924	        obtain ⟨rs, hlen, hmem, hreg⟩ := exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal R
1925	        apply le_sSup
1926	        refine ⟨rs, ?_, hmem, hreg⟩
1927	        exact_mod_cast hlen
1928	    rw [h1]
1929	    -- Final coercion: `((n : ℕ∞) : WithBot ℕ∞) = ((n : ℕ) : WithBot ℕ∞)`
1930	    -- is the standard `Nat.cast`-tower commutation.
1931	    rfl
1932	
1933	end CohenMacaulay
1934	
1935	end RingTheory

/-
Copyright (c) 2025 Nailin Guan. All rights reserved.
Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
Authors: Nailin Guan, Yongle Hu
-/
module

public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.TorsionFree
public import Mathlib.RingTheory.KrullDimension.Module
public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Regular.RegularSequence
public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Spectrum.Prime.LTSeries

/-!

# Krull Dimension of quotient regular sequence

## Main results

- `Module.supportDim_add_length_eq_supportDim_of_isRegular`: If `M` is a finite module over a
  Noetherian local ring `R`, `r₁, …, rₙ` is an `M`-sequence, then
  `dim M/(r₁, …, rₙ)M + n = dim M`.
-/

public section

namespace Module

variable {R : Type*} [CommRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
  {M : Type*} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [Module.Finite R M]

open RingTheory Sequence IsLocalRing Ideal PrimeSpectrum Pointwise

omit [IsNoetherianRing R] [Module.Finite R M] in
lemma exists_ltSeries_support_isMaximal_last_of_ltSeries_support (q : LTSeries (support R M)) :
    ∃ p : LTSeries (support R M), q.length ≤ p.length ∧ p.last.1.1.IsMaximal := by
  obtain ⟨m, hmm, hm⟩ := exists_le_maximal _ q.last.1.2.1
  obtain hlt | rfl := lt_or_eq_of_le hm
  · use q.snoc ⟨⟨m, inferInstance⟩, mem_support_mono hm q.last.2⟩ hlt
    simpa
  · use q

theorem supportDim_le_supportDim_quotSMulTop_succ_of_mem_jacobson {x : R}
    (h : x ∈ (annihilator R M).jacobson) : supportDim R M ≤ supportDim R (QuotSMulTop x M) + 1 := by
  nontriviality M
  refine iSup_le_iff.mpr (fun p ↦ ?_)
  wlog hxp : x ∈ p.last.1.1 generalizing p
  · obtain ⟨p, hle, hm⟩ := exists_ltSeries_support_isMaximal_last_of_ltSeries_support p
    have hj : (annihilator R M).jacobson ≤ p.last.1.1 :=
      sInf_le ⟨mem_support_iff_of_finite.mp p.last.2, inferInstance⟩
    exact (Nat.cast_le.mpr hle).trans <| this _ (hj h)
  -- `q` is a chain of primes such that `x ∈ q 1`, `p.length = q.length` and `p.head = q.head`.
  obtain ⟨q, hxq, hq, h0, _⟩ : ∃ q : LTSeries (PrimeSpectrum R), _ ∧ _ ∧ p.head = q.head ∧ _ :=
    exist_ltSeries_mem_one_of_mem_last (p.map Subtype.val (fun ⦃_ _⦄ lt ↦ lt)) hxp
  by_cases hp0 : p.length = 0
  · have hb : supportDim R (QuotSMulTop x M) ≠ ⊥ :=
      (supportDim_ne_bot_iff_nontrivial R (QuotSMulTop x M)).mpr <|
        Submodule.Quotient.nontrivial_iff.mpr <|
          (Submodule.top_ne_pointwise_smul_of_mem_jacobson_annihilator h).symm
    rw [hp0, ← WithBot.coe_unbot (supportDim R (QuotSMulTop x M)) hb]
    exact WithBot.coe_le_coe.mpr (zero_le ((supportDim R (QuotSMulTop x M)).unbot hb + 1))
  -- Let `q' i := q (i + 1)`, then `q'` is a chain of prime ideals in `Supp(M/xM)`.
  let q' : LTSeries (support R (QuotSMulTop x M)) := {
    length := p.length - 1
    toFun := by
      intro ⟨i, hi⟩
      have hi : i + 1 < q.length + 1 :=
        Nat.succ_lt_succ (hi.trans_eq ((Nat.sub_add_cancel (Nat.pos_of_ne_zero hp0)).trans hq))
      exact ⟨q ⟨i + 1, hi⟩, by simpa using
        ⟨mem_support_mono (by simpa [h0] using q.monotone (Fin.zero_le _)) p.head.2, q.monotone
          ((Fin.natCast_eq_mk (Nat.lt_of_add_left_lt hi)).trans_le (Nat.le_add_left 1 i)) hxq⟩⟩
    step := by exact fun _ ↦ q.strictMono (by simp)
  }
  grw [le_tsub_add (b := p.length) (a := 1), Nat.cast_add_one, supportDim, Order.krullDim,
    ← le_iSup _ q']

omit [IsNoetherianRing R] in
/-- If `M` is a finite module over a commutative ring `R`, `x ∈ M` is not in any minimal prime of
  `M`, then `dim M/xM + 1 ≤ dim M`. -/
theorem supportDim_quotSMulTop_succ_le_of_notMem_minimalPrimes {x : R}
    (hn : ∀ p ∈ (annihilator R M).minimalPrimes, x ∉ p) :
    supportDim R (QuotSMulTop x M) + 1 ≤ supportDim R M := by
  nontriviality M
  nontriviality (QuotSMulTop x M)
  have : Nonempty (Module.support R M) := nonempty_support_of_nontrivial.to_subtype
  have : Nonempty (Module.support R (QuotSMulTop x M)) := nonempty_support_of_nontrivial.to_subtype
  simp only [supportDim, Order.krullDim_eq_iSup_length]
  apply WithBot.coe_le_coe.mpr
  simp only [ENat.iSup_add, iSup_le_iff]
  intro p
  have hp := p.head.2
  simp only [support_quotSMulTop, Set.mem_inter_iff, mem_zeroLocus, Set.singleton_subset_iff] at hp
  have le : support R (QuotSMulTop x M) ⊆ support R M := by simp
  -- Since `Supp(M/xM) ⊆ Supp M`, `p` can be viewed as a chain of prime ideals in `Supp M`,
  -- which we denote by `q`.
  let q : LTSeries (support R M) :=
    p.map (Set.MapsTo.restrict id (support R (QuotSMulTop x M)) (support R M) le) (fun _ _ h ↦ h)
  obtain ⟨r, hrm, hr⟩ := exists_minimalPrimes_le (mem_support_iff_of_finite.mp q.head.2)
  let r : support R M := ⟨⟨r, minimalPrimes_isPrime hrm⟩, mem_support_iff_of_finite.mpr hrm.1.2⟩
  have hr : r < q.head := lt_of_le_of_ne hr (fun h ↦ hn q.head.1.1 (by rwa [← h]) hp.2)
  exact le_of_eq_of_le (by simp [q]) (le_iSup _ (q.cons r hr))

theorem supportDim_quotSMulTop_succ_eq_of_notMem_minimalPrimes_of_mem_jacobson {x : R}
    (hn : ∀ p ∈ (annihilator R M).minimalPrimes, x ∉ p) (hx : x ∈ (annihilator R M).jacobson) :
    supportDim R (QuotSMulTop x M) + 1 = supportDim R M :=
  le_antisymm (supportDim_quotSMulTop_succ_le_of_notMem_minimalPrimes hn)
    (supportDim_le_supportDim_quotSMulTop_succ_of_mem_jacobson hx)

theorem supportDim_quotSMulTop_succ_eq_supportDim_mem_jacobson {x : R} (reg : IsSMulRegular M x)
    (hx : x ∈ (annihilator R M).jacobson) : supportDim R (QuotSMulTop x M) + 1 = supportDim R M :=
  supportDim_quotSMulTop_succ_eq_of_notMem_minimalPrimes_of_mem_jacobson
    (fun _ ↦ reg.notMem_of_mem_minimalPrimes) hx

lemma _root_.ringKrullDim_quotSMulTop_succ_eq_ringKrullDim_of_mem_jacobson {x : R}
    (reg : IsSMulRegular R x) (hx : x ∈ Ring.jacobson R) :
    ringKrullDim (QuotSMulTop x R) + 1 = ringKrullDim R := by
  rw [← supportDim_quotient_eq_ringKrullDim, ← supportDim_self_eq_ringKrullDim]
  exact supportDim_quotSMulTop_succ_eq_supportDim_mem_jacobson reg
    ((annihilator R R).ringJacobson_le_jacobson hx)

lemma _root_.ringKrullDim_quotient_span_singleton_succ_eq_ringKrullDim_of_mem_jacobson {x : R}
    (reg : IsSMulRegular R x) (hx : x ∈ Ring.jacobson R) :
    ringKrullDim (R ⧸ span {x}) + 1 = ringKrullDim R := by
  have h : span {x} = x • (⊤ : Ideal R) := by simp [← Submodule.ideal_span_singleton_smul]
  rw [ringKrullDim_eq_of_ringEquiv (quotientEquivAlgOfEq R h).toRingEquiv,
    ringKrullDim_quotSMulTop_succ_eq_ringKrullDim_of_mem_jacobson reg hx]

/-- If `r` is a nonzerodivisor contained in an ideal of maximal height,
`dim (R / (r)) + 1 = dim R`. -/
lemma ringKrullDim_quotient_add_one_of_mem_nonZeroDivisors {r : R} (hr : r ∈ nonZeroDivisors R)
    {p : Ideal R} [p.IsPrime] (h : p.height = ringKrullDim R) (hp : r ∈ p) :
    ringKrullDim (R ⧸ span {r}) + 1 = ringKrullDim R := by
  refine le_antisymm (ringKrullDim_quotient_succ_le_of_nonZeroDivisor hr) (h ▸ ?_)
  exact Ideal.height_le_ringKrullDim_quotient_add_one hp

variable [IsLocalRing R]

/-- If `M` is a finite module over a Noetherian local ring `R`, then `dim M ≤ dim M/xM + 1`
  for every `x` in the maximal ideal of the local ring `R`. -/
@[stacks 0B52 "the second inequality"]
theorem supportDim_le_supportDim_quotSMulTop_succ {x : R} (hx : x ∈ maximalIdeal R) :
    supportDim R M ≤ supportDim R (QuotSMulTop x M) + 1 :=
  supportDim_le_supportDim_quotSMulTop_succ_of_mem_jacobson ((maximalIdeal_le_jacobson _) hx)

lemma _root_.ringKrullDim_le_ringKrullDim_quotSMulTop_succ {x : R} (hx : x ∈ maximalIdeal R) :
    ringKrullDim R ≤ ringKrullDim (R ⧸ x • (⊤ : Ideal R)) + 1 := by
  rw [← Module.supportDim_self_eq_ringKrullDim, ← Module.supportDim_quotient_eq_ringKrullDim]
  exact supportDim_le_supportDim_quotSMulTop_succ hx

content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
